result:
spaghetti happens when names lie and boundaries blur. few moves that help:

name things for what they do, not how. if you rename function and three callers still make sense, name was good.

one function, one job. if you write "and" in docstring ("parses and validates and saves"), split it.

keep data flow one-directional. input comes in top, transforms step by step, result leaves bottom. no reaching back up to mutate caller's state.

separate pure logic from i/o. pure functions (no disk, no network, no clock) go in one place. side-effectful wrappers go in another. makes both easier to read and test.

delete dead code aggressively. unused branches, commented-out blocks, "might need later" helpers — all add weight. git remembers.

flatten nesting. early returns beat nested ifs. guard clauses at top, happy path unindented below.

stop passing god-objects around. if function takes `ctx` with 14 fields but uses 2, pass those 2.

when lost, draw the call graph on paper. 10 minutes sketching reveals which module is doing too much.

and smallest trick: read your own code out loud tomorrow. anything that makes you pause is the spaghetti.
